answer: Tier 1 = $7,500 per calendar day after Substantial Completion date. Tier 2 = $1,000 per calendar day if Final Completion is not achieved (30)days after Substantial Completion. Tier 3 = $500 per calendar day if model units are not completed within (120)days of Substantial Completion date.

question_text: What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
answer: First-tier - Substantial Completion - the Owner may assess and the Contractor shall then be liable for payment of First-tier Liquidated Damages in the amount of $7,500 per calendar day for each day the actual date of Substantial Completion of the Work exceeds the scheduled date of Substantial Completion set forth in Section 4.3 Second-Tier - Final Completion - After the actual date of Substantial Completion of the Work, the Owner will continue to suffer financial loss if the Contractor fails to achieve Final Completion within thirty (30) days after the actual date of Substantial Completion. If the Contractor fails to achieve Final Completion within thirty (30) days after the actual date of Substantial Completion, the Owner may assess and the Contractor shall then be liable for payment of Second-tier Liquidated Damages in the amount of $1,000 per calendar day for each day the actual date of Final Completion exceeds thirty (30) days after the actual date of Substantial Completion. Third-tier - Model Units - These Model Units shall be completed, permitted for occupancy and usable for Owner's intended purpose at least one hundred twenty (120) days prior to the projected date of Substantial Completion of the Work (the "Model Unit Milestone"). If the Contractor fails to complete the Model Units by the Model Unit Milestone the Owner may assess and the Contractor shall then be liable for payment of Model Unit Milestone Liquidated Damages in the amount of $500 per calendar day for each day the actual completion of the Model Units exceeds the Model Unit Milestone.

question_text: What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
answer: Article 6.1.6: Insert the following: “Liquidated Damages. The Construction Manager agrees that it will pay to the Owner, as liquidated damages for delay, the sum of Five Hundred Dollars ($500.00) for each calendar day after the date scheduled for Substantial Completion of the Work until the Contractor achieves Substantial Completion of the Work. Such liquidated damages are agreed to by the Construction Manager, not as a penalty, but as a reasonable forecast of the substantial damages that the Owner will incur should Substantial Completion of the Work be delayed, and in view of the agreed difficulty of accurately specifying and ascertaining such delay damages. Such liquidated damages shall be Owner’s sole monetary remedy for delay in achieving Substantial Completion of the Work unless such liquidated damages are deemed to be an unenforceable penalty, in which case Owner shall be entitled to its actual delay damages. Imposition of such liquidated damages shall not relieve the Construction Manager from its obligation to achieve the date of Substantial Completion as required by the Contract Documents, nor limit the Owner’s non-monetary remedies, such as termination for default. Owner may deduct all such liquidated damages from the Construction Manager’s Applications for Payment or make take any such other action(s) Owner may deem necessary to enforce its entitlement to such liquidated damages against the Construction Manager and its Surety, if any.”

answer: 11.3.1 Owner shall purchase and maintain Builder’s Risk Property insurance upon the entire Work at the Site in an amount not less than the full insurable completed value thereof. This insurance will remain in force from the commencement of the Work until the date the completed Work is acceptable by Owner. This insurance shall cover the interest of Owner, and shall name Construction Manager as an additional insured. This insurance will insure against the perils of fire, extended coverage, theft, vandalism and malicious mischief and whatever other so-called “All Risk” perils Owner shall deem appropriate. This insurance shall apply only to the Work and materials delivered to and suitably stored at the Site. The risk of loss or damage to materials, supplies or other property away from the Site, in storage, while being fabricated, or in transit to the Site is that of Construction Manager and will not be covered by this insurance. This insurance will not include coverage for tools, machinery and equipment that are owned by, the responsibility of, or in the care, custody or control of Construction Manager, Subcontractors or Sub-Subcontractors. This insurance may include a deductible amount of not more than $5,000 or whatever mandatory higher deductibles are required by the insurance underwriter or are selected by Owner. Any loss or damage sustained to the Work prior to completion and acceptance by Owner that is not covered by this insurance due to the operation of the deductible clause shall be the responsibility of Owner and included in the “Cost of the Work”; provided, however, that Construction Manager shall be responsible for the deductible amount (up to a $5,000 deductible) in connection with losses or damage to materials stored on Site caused by theft or vandalism. Any claims for such theft or vandalism up to $5,000 shall not be recognized by Owner.

In addition, upon request Owner shall confirm to Construction Manager’s bond issuer upon its request, the availability of funding for the Project. If Owner shall require such bonds, Construction Manager shall obtain and at all times thereafter during the performance of the Work, maintain a performance bond and a labor and material payment bond, each with a dual obligee rider naming Owner as obligee thereunder (and, if required by Owner, naming Owner’s lender(s) as an obligee(s) thereunder). The performance and payment bonds shall be in such forms as Owner shall reasonably require. In lieu of providing such bonds, Construction Manager may deliver to Owner an irrevocable letter of credit issued for Owner’s benefit by a bank satisfactory to Owner, in an amount not less than the Guaranteed Maximum Price and with a term of not less than twenty-four (24) months or such shorter period of time approved by Owner and Owner’s lender. question_text: What are the Terms & Conditions / IMC's obligations for Design Document Review?
answer: Contractor shall review carefully and verify all Contract Documents as required by Section 2.3 of the agreement and this Section 3.2 and report discrepancies, inconsistencies or errors discovered by Contractor to the Architect and owner, in writing, in ample time so that corrections, if any, may be made without delaying the work or involving additional expense. Figures on drawings take precedence over measurements by scale. Scaling is done at the Contractor's own risk. Figures and notations on large scale drawings shall take precedence over small scale drawings. Material, sizes, notations and descriptions in specifications take precedence over drawings. Should Contractor fail to notify Owner and Architect of any discrepancies, inconsistencies or errors that it is required to report to Owner and Architect under this Section 3 .2.3, Contractor is responsible, at its own expense, to make any corrections or perform any Work arising from such failures to notify Owner and Architect. GMP assumes that the Owner’s Design Team has coordinated the Design between different design disciplines. GMP assumes the Architect will be responsible to coordinate all the design disciplines to resolve design conflicts among the Contract Documents, except for any design conflicts pertaining to design that has been delegated IMC and/or its Subcontractors.

question_text: What are the GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions?
answer: "Buy-Out Savings" shall mean the difference between (i) the sum of the subcontract amounts in the schedule of values attached as Exhibit C and (ii) the sum of the actual amounts of the subcontracts entered into by the Contractor for the performance of the Work. Any Buy-Out Savings shall be shared by the Contractor and Owner as follows: Thirty percent (30%) to the Owner and seventy percent (70%) to the Contractor for Cost Savings declared by Contractor in months one (l) through three (3) of the Agreement term. Forty percent (40%) to the Owner and sixty percent (60%) to the Contractor for Cost Savings declared by Contractor in months four (4) through six (6) of the Agreement term. Fifty percent (50%) to the Owner and fifty percent (50%) to the Contractor for Cost Savings determined via final project cost audit or accounting. Upon declaration of the Buy-Out Savings, the Contractor shall provide the Owner with documentation reasonably requested by Owner to substantiate the Buy-Out Savings. The percentage of the Buy-Out Savings allocated to the Owner according to the foregoing schedule shall be subtracted from the Guaranteed Maximum Price through a deductive Change Order to be executed by the parties promptly after the declaration of the Buy-Out Savings. The portion of the Buy-Out Savings allocated to the Contractor shall be added to the Contractor's Fee, and become eligible for billing in accordance with Article 12 hereof promptly after the execution of the aforementioned Change Order. Any Cost Savings shall be shared by the Contractor and Owner as follows: Fifty percent (50%) to the Owner and fifty percent (50%) to the Contractor "Cost Savings" shall mean the difference between the (l) Cost of the Work plus the Contractor's Fee, and (2) the Guaranteed Maximum Price, as modified.

Should the actual final Cost of the Work plus the Design Builder’s Fee be less than the GMP amount as adjusted pursuant to this Contract (“Savings”), the Savings shall accrue one hundred percent (100%) to Princeton University and zero percent (0%) to the Design Builder. Should, however, the actual final Cost of the Work plus the Design Builder’s Fee applicable thereto exceed the GMP amount as adjusted pursuant to this Contract, then, the excess amount shall be borne solely by the Design Builder.

question_text: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
answer: GMP Part VI, paragraph f - Section 2 - Page 8 Subcontractor Buyout: Upon completion of 80% by count of the Subcontractor buyouts, Design Builder shall prepare and submit a “Subcontractor Buyout Report” for Princeton University’s review and approval that lists and summarizes all Subcontractor buyouts to date. For each Subcontract awarded, the report shall include a list of all trade line items included in the award and compare the total GMP amount for those line items to the actual award value for the Subcontract to calculate the “buyout variance” for each Subcontract and then tally these to determine if there is an overall net buyout “savings” or “overrun” at that point. If there are net buyout “savings” then that net amount shall be transferred into and treated as a part of the Construction Contingency. Should there be a net buyout “overrun” a transfer out of Construction Contingency may be made after consideration of other mitigating actions that may be available within the GMP. It is expressly understood that unused Allowances shall not for any purpose be transferred into the Construction Contingency and shall remain as a credit to Princeton University one hundred percent (100%) of such.

question_text: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
answer: 9.2.1 Owner agrees to reimburse Construction Manager for the Cost of the Work pursuant to this Article 9. In full consideration of the full and complete performance of the Work and all other Construction Phase obligations of Construction Manager hereunder, Owner shall pay to Construction Manager a sum of money equal to the “Contract Sum”. The Contract Sum is the total of (i) the Cost of the Work, as hereinafter defined, and (ii) Construction Manager’s Fee, as hereinafter defined, subject to adjustment as provided in this Agreement. The Contract Sum shall not exceed a “Guaranteed Maximum Price” as hereinafter established pursuant to the terms of this Section. Once so established, the Guaranteed Maximum Price shall not be adjusted except as expressly provided in this Agreement. Owner and Construction Manager hereby agree the Guaranteed Maximum Price will be established when the Drawings and Specifications are further developed, and at such time, Owner and Construction Manager shall execute one or more amendments to this Agreement to confirm the actual Guaranteed Maximum Price for the Project (each, a “Guaranteed Maximum Price Amendment”). Once the actual Guaranteed Maximum Price is established for all or a portion of the Project, a line item breakdown of the Guaranteed Maximum Price for such scope of Work shall be attached hereto as Exhibit “H”. Construction Manager may reallocate proven savings in one line item to overages in another, subject to Owner’s prior approval, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, by Change Order modifying Exhibit “H”. Construction Manager must notify Owner in a monthly budget report of all proposed reallocations it being understood that Owner reserves the right to disapprove any and all reallocations for expenditures that would be considered unreasonably excessive for General Conditions or Work performed directly by Construction Manager and not delegated to Subcontractors. “Construction M’s Fee” is to be an amount equal to two and three quarters percent (2.75%) of the Cost of the Work excluding the amount allocated for General Conditions. Owner and Construction Manager hereby acknowledge and agree that based upon the proposed Project budget as of the date hereof, the General Conditions costs, which are defined as those categories of cost listed on Exhibit “I” attached hereto (the “General Conditions”), the estimated amount allocated for General Conditions shall be established by Owner and Construction Manager and set forth in a Guaranteed Maximum Price Amendment. The estimated amount of the General Conditions set in a Guaranteed Maximum Price Amendment shall be subject to adjustment upon the mutual agreement of Owner (not to be unreasonably withheld) and Construction Manager as the Project progresses.

answer: SECTION 4.05 Buy Out Savings. Except in the case of a replacement pursuant to Section 2.03(h) above, if General Contractor enters into a Subcontract after execution of the Agreement, General Contractor shall update the Schedule of Values to reflect the actual Subcontract price for that scope of work. If the Subcontract price is less than the value attributed to such Work in the Schedule of Values, the difference shall be referred to as “Buy Out Savings.” If the Subcontract price is more than the value attributed to such Work in the Schedule of Values, the difference shall be referred to as “Buy Out Losses.” The Contingency shall be increased or decreased, as applicable, by the net amount of Buy Out Savings, after accounting for all Buy Out Losses, and the Schedule of Values shall be updated accordingly; provided, however, that in no event shall the GMP be increased on account of any Buy Out Losses.

question_text: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
answer: GMP Part VI, paragraph f - Page 7 Construction Contingency. A “Construction Contingency” shall be included within the Guaranteed Maximum Price in the amount of $283,002 (as also identified the GMP Breakdown and Basis Documents) which shall be reserved to cover increased or added costs that are Cost of the Work resulting from circumstances that may not be completely predictable or foreseeable at the time that the GMP is established but that can reasonably be assumed to be included within the scope of Work for the Project and presumed to have been included in but were omitted from the GMP. Examples of circumstances that may give rise to the use of Construction Contingency include but are not limited to: (i) utility coordination by the Design Builder or a Subcontractor at any tier; (ii) purchasing, estimating, and scheduling difficulties (e.g., changes in local material or labor market conditions at time of bidding; or, items that were missed in the GMP but that are required expressly or by necessary implication by the Contract Documents for a complete Project; or, net Subcontractor buyout overruns that exceed the GMP for the trade line(s) involved as provided in subpart (2) to this paragraph (f)) below); (iii) detail refinement (i.e., minor items or material selections required to complete a detail that may have not been fully developed in the Design Documents or that were familiar to the Design Builder from its work on previous Princeton University Projects); and (iv) the re-procurement of a Subcontractor at any tier due to termination for any reason, excluding any re-procurement costs that would be covered by Subcontractor Default Insurance (SDI) and any deductibles to be paid in connection with any applicable SDI insurance, if SDI is included in this Contract, and any legal costs which are unallowable. The above list is meant to be illustrative and Princeton University, in its discretion, may determine that there are other circumstances that meet the general definition for use of Construction Contingency; however, the Construction Contingency is not intended to fund additional scope or to fund additional cost of allowances beyond the dollar values included in the GMP. Additionally, notwithstanding anything to the contrary herein, Design Builder shall not be allowed to receive payment of sums from the Construction Contingency or otherwise for costs/charges/sums/etc. relating to: (i) sums that are successfully recovered from a Subcontractor or other responsible entity (with the express understanding that Design Builder shall be required to take reasonable action to recover such sums without resort to litigation); (ii) sums for which Design Builder would have been indemnified or compensated by insurance (including SDI), if not for the failure of Design Builder to procure and maintain such insurance in accordance with the requirements of this Contract, or if not for the failure of Design Builder to comply with the requirements of any insurance carriers providing coverage for the Project, or if not for the failure of Design Builder to pursue all available claims against such carriers for coverage or recovery; (iii) deductibles of any kind (insurance, SDI, etc.); and (iv) any sums, costs, charges, etc. that exceed the available Construction Contingency set forth in the Guaranteed Maximum Price (as such amount may be adjusted only by the terms of this Contract). Notwithstanding the establishment of the Construction Contingency or any of the listed examples for its potential usage above or any other circumstances, the Design Builder’s use of any Construction Contingency funds and each individual transfer into or out of Construction Contingency shall be subject to advance review and written approval by Princeton University, which shall not be unreasonably withheld. If the Construction Contingency is exhausted, all costs and expenses that would qualify for reimbursement from the Construction Contingency shall be borne by Design

question_text: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
answer: The Contingency shall be an amount available to reimburse the Contractor for work not identified or quantified by the Contractor in its cost estimate, such as: (a) estimating and purchasing errors; (b) premium time to maintain the progress of the Work in accordance with the Project schedule due to an action or inaction by the Contractor or any Subcontractor (provided that the Contractor shall require Subcontractors to absorb in their base Subcontracts all premium time due to failure of the Subcontractor to properly man the Project or organize the Work); (c) unforeseen construction problems (except to the extent that, as a contractor experienced in the construction of projects similar to the Project, the Contractor should have foreseen such problems); (d) ordinary or seasonal weather conditions; and (e) Work identified or reasonably inferable from the Contract Documents, but not completely detailed in such documents. For purposes hereof, “reasonably inferable” shall mean reasonably necessary in order to complete any system or finished product. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary herein contained: (i) the Contingency may only be used for costs which are included in the Cost of the Work, as defined in Section 7.1, (ii) the Contingency may not be used for costs which are excluded from the Cost of the Work, as set forth in Section 8.1, (iii) INTENTIONALLY DELETED (iv) the Contingency may not be used to pay for costs due to unforeseen conditions (subject to other conditions of this Contract or the A201 General Conditions), overages in “Allowances” or Scope Changes, all of which shall be subject to Change Orders in accordance with Article 6, and (v) the Contractor shall be required to obtain the Owner’s written consent in advance of incurring costs during any calendar month in excess of twenty-five thousand dollars ($25,000.00) that the Contractor proposes be charged to the Contingency. The Owner agrees that it shall not unreasonably withhold or delay its consent to any request from the Contractor to properly use the Contingency pursuant to clauses (iii), (iv) or (v) above, recognizing that the Owner will collaborate with the Contractor in order to enable the Contractor to achieve the Project schedule. In order to enable the Owner to monitor the Contractor’s compliance with the provisions of this Section 5.2.5, the Contractor shall, commencing as of the execution and delivery of this Contract and continuing until Final Completion: (a) submit to the Owner a detailed monthly (or more frequently, if the Owner has a reasonable basis for requiring the same more frequently) Contingency log and projection for review by the Owner of all Contingency expenditures, and (b) prior to incurring any cost which may be charged to the Contingency in excess of twenty-five thousand dollars ($25,000.00), the Contractor shall submit a written request to the Owner for reimbursement of such cost, together with an explanation of the reason such cost is to be incurred.

question_text: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
answer: The Owner acknowledges that the Guaranteed Maximum Price Proposal may include an amount, not to exceed ___ percent (__%) of the Direct Cost of the Work (the "Contingency"), intended to cover refinement of details of design within the scope and standards of quality and quantities on which the Construction Budget was based; costs arising during construction that were not anticipated or not adequately anticipated by Construction Manager at the time of preparing the Guaranteed Maximum Price Proposal; errors in estimating; costs due to omission of construction related items from bid packages; cost overruns resulting from variations between bids and budgeted amounts; correcting minor defects in the Work if the party responsible therefor cannot be determined; and costs, exclusive of legal fees unless agreed to otherwise in writing at the Owner’s sole discretion, resulting from disputes with Subcontractors (hereinafter referred to as “Contingency Costs”). Construction Manager may charge sums (without any markup for Construction Manager’s Fee) to Contingency for the reasons set forth in this Paragraph, provided (1) the costs therefore are identified as reimbursable in Article 6 of this Agreement; (2) the Construction Manager has submitted detailed documentation supporting the amounts requested; and (3) the Construction Manager has received the prior written approval of the Owner for any item which may cost more than $5,000.00. The Construction Manager will track all items pending, obligated and spent against the Contingency and provide a written report to the Owner with each monthly Application for Payment and as otherwise requested by the Owner. When the Guaranteed Maximum Price is agreed upon by the parties the Contingency will be converted to a fixed dollar-sum and in no event shall the Contingency increase after the acceptance of the Guaranteed Maximum Price Proposal. Construction Manager shall bear all costs described in this Paragraph in excess of the Contingency.”

question_text: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
answer: The GMP includes a Contingency fund for unknown and unforeseeable risks that are not included directly or by inference in the SOV and the costs of which are not directly or by inference precluded by and previsions of the Agreement. The construction manager shall not make any distribution in excess of $25,000 from the contingency fund without owner's prior written approval which shall not be unreasonably withheld. The contingency fund may be used for items such as unanticipated local market conditions, material price increases, labor and materials conditions, bidder and subcontractor defaults, construction managers estimating error, and interfacing omissions between and from the various work categories. The contingency fund may not be used to correct defective work or other negligent acts of the construction manager or its subcontractors or suppliers, except to the extent permissible under section 7.7.3.

question_text: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
answer: The Contingency, when agreed upon by the Owner and the Contractor as set forth below and subject to the conditions in this Section 5.2.5, shall be an amount available to reimburse the Contractor for work not reasonably identified or reasonably quantified by the Contractor in its cost estimate, such as: (a) estimating and purchasing errors; (b) premium time to maintain the progress of the Work in accordance with the Project schedule due to an action or inaction by the Contractor or any Subcontractor (provided that the Contractor shall require Subcontractors to absorb in their base Subcontracts all premium time due to failure of the Subcontractor to properly man the Project or organize the Work); (c) unforeseen construction problems (except to the extent that, as a contractor experienced in the construction of projects similar to the Project, the Contractor should have foreseen such problems); (d) extrordinary or unusual seasonal weather conditions; and (e) Work not identified or reasonably inferable from the Contract Documents, but not completely detailed in such documents.

question_text: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
answer: 9.2.1.4 The “Contingency” provided in the Guaranteed Maximum Price plus accumulated line item savings, that Owner agrees to allow Contractor to utilize, are the maximum sums available to cover costs that would increase the Cost of the Work and result from unforeseen conditions and events not evident to Owner or Construction Manager at the time of execution of this Agreement, and that would not constitute a Change Order under Article 7, such as (i) refinement of details of design within the scope and standard of quality and quantities on which the Guaranteed Maximum Cost was based, (ii) Intentionally Deleted, (iii) correction of minor defects or omissions in the Work (which have not resulted from the negligence of Construction Manager or anyone acting through or under Construction Manager), and (iv) delays due to labor disputes and unusually severe weather that would not give rise to a Change Order under Subsection 8.3.1. The proposed use of the Contingency shall be submitted by Contractor and decided upon on a monthly basis by Owner. No sums may be charged to the Contingency except upon approval by Owner, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, and then only to the extent that such funds have been paid, or are to be paid, by Construction Manager. If, at the completion of the Work any sums remain in the Contingency, such sums shall not constitute “savings” and, accordingly, Construction Manager shall not be entitled to share in any portion thereof.

answer: 8.3.5 Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in any of the Contract Documents, the Contractor acknowledges and agrees that no extension of time shall be granted on account of adverse weather conditions except as provided for in this paragraph. For purposes of this Agreement, "adverse weather" is defined as when because of weather conditions: (1) a state emergency or other declaration is made by the Governor of Pennsylvania directing non-essential workers to not report to work; or (2) Work cannot safely progress including, without limitation, winds that exceed the applicable manufacturer’s recommendation or local regulation for the safe use of a hoist, crane or other equipment (3) such weather conditons exist that are are unordinary and that significantly hinder the performance of the Work. For Non-Chargeable Delays in excess of each instance and/or the aggregate provided in the preceding sentence, Contactor’s claim for cost reimbursement shall be capped at Twenty-Five Thousand Dollars ($25,000.00). In the event that Contractor disputes any determination by Princeton University that Contractor is obligated to accelerate the Work at Contractor’s expense, provided that Princeton University has issued a Disputed Change Order pursuant to the applicable Clauses of Section H hereof, Contractor shall proceed with such acceleration. (b) Acceleration at Princeton University’s Expense. Princeton University may request the Contractor to accelerate (including more personnel, overtime, and/or double shifts) the completion of the Work or a portion of the Work, at a time when the Contractor is not behind the Project Schedule in the performance of the Work. The Contractor agrees to accelerate the Work at Princeton University’s request, and notwithstanding anything in the Contract to the contrary, the Contractor shall be reimbursed only for the Contractor’s extra labor cost over the amount for regular time during the period of such acceleration, including additional fringe benefit costs, and insurance and taxes incurred by it (unless otherwise not reimbursable by some other provision of the Contract) with respect thereto and only those other actual costs of the Contractor directly related to the acceleration, which have been approved in advance by Princeton University in a written signed Change Order. Time tickets covering said overtime must be correlated to the accelerated/overtime schedule activities and submitted to Princeton University for checking and approval. No Fee, if Contractor otherwise receives a Fee on this Contract, nor additional overhead nor any profit is to be charged by or allowed to Contractor on account of acceleration or costs related thereto, nor shall Contractor be compensated for any lost efficiency or production alleged to have resulted from said overtime work, except as expressly agreed in advance by Princeton University in a written signed Change Order. (c) Princeton University may exercise the rights furnished to it under the Clause E4 as frequently as it deems necessary or favorable. question_text: What are the Substantial Completion Terms & Conditions?
answer: The term "Substantial Completion" of the Work shall mean when the Work is determined by the Owner to be complete in accordance with the Contract Documents, such that all interior and exterior construction, including without limitation, all components, systems, facilities, amenities, common areas, and portions of the Work, have been completed and is habitable, operational, and useable for the Owner's intended purposes (such that the units are fully leasable or saleable and the occupants of the units will have full use and enjoyment of all aspects and amenities), except for incidental corrective work, cleaning beyond the cleaning needed for Owner's intended use (which may remain for Final Completion), and minor commissioning and balancing work which may take place in conjunction with the Owner's set-up for operations activities for the Project. The Contractor acknowledges that the issuance of Use and Occupancy permits are a condition precedent to the Substantial Completion of the Work unless withheld by no fault of the Contractor; provided, however, that, for the purposes of First-tier Liquidated Damages, if all conditions have been met and the local City, County or Township authority is ready to issue Use and Occupancy permits and the Owner has not issued written approval to Contractor to obtain said permits, then the Contractor shall not be assessed First-tier Liquidated Damages for failure to obtain Use and Occupancy permits. The Contractor further acknowledges that (i) Owner is relying on the date of Substantial Completion established by Contractor to coordinate Owner's turnover and operations activities for the Project, and (ii) Owner may suffer substantial loss if the Contractor is unable to achieve Substantial Completion on or prior to the date of Substantial Completion established by the Contractor. Accordingly, at least ninety (90) days prior to the date on which Contractor shall achieve Substantial Completion of an assisted living community, or at least one hundred twenty (120) days prior to the date on which Contractor shall achieve Substantial Completion of a continuum community, Contractor shall complete, execute and deliver to Owner the Substantial Completion Date Certificate in the form attached to the Agreement as Exhibit M. When the Contractor considers the Work or a designated portion of the Work to be sufficiently complete in accordance with the above, the Contractor shall comply with the following procedures for acceptance of the Work or designated portion of the Work by the Owner.

(5) Training. All training required by the Contract Documents is complete unless exempted by Princeton University. (6) As-Built Drawings that accurately depict the installed condition of all Work must be posted to the Capital Project Management System. (10) Project Card Access & Campus Security (CACS). Work is complete, fully operational and has been electronically accepted through the Princeton University ATP commissioning. Security systems and/or exterior CACS doors labels are installed. (11) Cleaning. The Project is clean. The interior and exterior of building (including windows) and site are free of dirt, dust, and debris. Princeton University has accepted the Project as clean and agrees to commence housekeeping activities on a mutually agreeable date (12) Contractor has complied with all other requirements in the Contract Documents that are a condition of Substantial Completion or are required by Princeton University to be completed or performed by Contractor as a condition of achieving Substantial Completion.

answer: A201 9.10.2 Neither final payment nor any remaining retained percentage shall become due until the Contractor submits to the Architect (1) an affidavit that payrolls, bills for materials and equipment, and other indebtedness connected with the Work for which the Owner or the Owner’s property might be responsible or encumbered (less amounts withheld by Owner) have been paid or otherwise satisfied, (2) a certificate evidencing that insurance required by the Contract Documents to remain in force after final payment is currently in effect and will not be canceled or allowed to expire until at least 30 days’ prior written notice has been given to the Owner, (3) a written statement that the Contractor knows of no substantial reason that the insurance will not be renewable to cover the period required by the Contract Documents, (4) consent of surety, if any, to final payment; (5) a marked up set of drawings including all changes in the Work made during the construction process, and (6) other data establishing payment or satisfaction of obligations, such as receipts, releases and waivers of liens, claims, security interests or encumbrances arising out of the Contract, from Contractor, all Subcontractors and anyone else who could create or file a lien with respect to the Project Site under applicable law if not paid the full amount to which such entity is entitled in connection with the Work, to the extent and in such form as may be designated by the Owner. If a Subcontractor refuses to furnish a release or waiver required by the Owner, the Contractor may furnish a bond satisfactory to the Owner to indemnify the Owner against such lien. If such lien is filed or remains unsatisfied after payments are made, the Contractor shall indemnify and promptly pay to the Owner or any other person or entity all money that the Owner may be compelled to pay in discharging such lien, including all costs and reasonable attorneys’ fees. 9.10.3 As a condition precedent to final payment, the Contractor shall also submit to the Owner (a) original guarantees and warranties obtained by the Contractor with respect to the Work, properly indexed and placed in a loose-leaf binder, (b) complete installation, operation and maintenance manuals relating to equipment and materials used in the Work, including, without limitation, all manufacturer’s literature, properly indexed and placed in a loose-leaf binder, (c) all inspection reports, permits and certificates of occupancy and other licenses necessary for the occupancy of the Project, properly indexed and placed in a loose-leaf binder, (d) a certificate evidencing that al insurance required by the Contract Documents shall remain in full force and effect after final payment is made, and that such insurance will not be cancelled or terminated until at least thirty (30) days prior written notice has been given to the Owner and the Owner’s lender, if any, and (e) any and all other items required to be submitted by the Contractor pursuant to the Contract Documents. The requirements that must be satisfied for final payments as described in this Subparagraph 9.10.3 shall apply to final payment upon final completion of all of the Work and final payment which is to occur under this Contract prior thereto as a result of the termination of this Contract by either party in accordance herewith. question_text: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
answer: if the owner does not pay the Contractor within seven days after the date established in the Contract Documents the undisputed amounts due or awarded by binding dispute resolution, then the Contractor may, upon seven additional days, written notice to the owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the undisputed amount owing has been received. Such cessation of Work shall be deemed to be an Excusable Delay under the provisions of Section 8.3 and the Contract Time and/or Contract Sum shall be adjusted to the extent permitted by Section 8.3 Notwithstanding the foregoing, in the event the unpaid amount owing to Contractor is less than 5% of the total amount of the approved advance, then Contractor shall not have the right to stop the Work unless such unpaid amount remaining outstanding for forty-five (45) days after the date for payment established in the Contract Documents and then only upon seven additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect.

question_text: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
answer: If the Architect does not issue a Certificate for Payment for which the Owner and Contractor agree that an undisputed amount is due, through no fault of the Contractor, within forty-five (45) days after receipt of the Contractor's Application for Payment, or if the Owner does not pay the Contractor within seven (7) days after the date established in the Contract Documents the amount certified by the Architect, then the Contractor may, upon seven (7) additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the amount owing has been received. The Owner shall compensate the Contractor for the reasonable costs of shutdown and start-up, but this provision and such compensation shall nonetheless not be deemed to conflict with the “No Damages for Delay” provision set forth in the Contract, but shall be read in conjunction therewith. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the Owner and Contractor have a payment dispute, the Contractor shall proceed with the Work without delay, subject to Contractor’s right to assert a claim in accordance herein.

question_text: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?

question_text: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?

Provided the Contractor’s election to stop the Work was proper, the Contract Time shall be extended appropriately and the Contract Sum shall be increased by the amount of the Contractor’s reasonable costs incurred in connection with shut-down, delay and start-up, plus interest as provided for in the Contract Documents. question_text: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
answer: If the Work is stopped for a period of ninety (90) consecutive days (and such stoppage was not contemplated in the construction schedule) through no breach of contract, act or fault of the Contractor or a Subcontractor, Sub-subcontractor or their agents or employees or any other persons or entities performing portions of the Work under direct or indirect contract with the Contractor, because of : (a) the issuance of an order of a court or other public authority having jurisdiction that requires all Work to be stopped; or (b) an act of government, such as a declaration of national emergency that requires all Work to be stopped, then, upon fifteen (15) days’ prior written notice to the Owner of its intention to do so, the Contractor may declare a constructive termination for convenience of its employment under the Contract and receive from the Owner the compensation to which the Contractor is entitled under Section 14.1.3. answer: CLAUSE E12 FINAL COMPLETION AND FINAL PAYMENT (a) Contractor shall achieve final completion of the Work of the entire Project for the purpose of determining the time of Final Payment (as defined in Clause I3) when: all certificates and permits necessary for permanent occupancy of the Project by Princeton University have been obtained; all Final Punchlist Work has been completed and approved by Princeton University in its discretion; all warranties, guaranties, affidavits and waivers of liens and releases have been delivered to Princeton University; all obligations set forth in Clause F2 (As-Builts) and F3 (Operating and Maintenance Manuals) have been achieved; and all other obligations of Contractor under the Contract have been fully performed as determined by Princeton University for the limited purpose of determining the time when Final Payment may be made. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Clause E12 or the Contract Documents, Contractor shall be deemed to have reached final completion if it has achieved all other requirements for final completion but for the failure to secure a permanent certificate of occupancy for the Project if such failure is through no fault of the Contractor (or those for whom the Contractor is responsible) and outside the reasonable control of Contractor. By determining that Contractor has achieved final completion for the purpose of authorizing Final Payment, Princeton University does not waive any rights, Claims (as defined in Clause J3) and/or other claims, remedies, or defenses under the Contract in any manner whatsoever. The Final Payment will then be made in accordance with the payment provisions of this Contract. (b) Other than as specifically provided in this Contract, neither any progress payment nor Final Payment to the Contractor, nor partial or full utilization or occupancy of the Work for any purpose by Princeton University, nor any other act or omission by Princeton University shall be interpreted or construed as an acceptance of any Work which is not strictly in compliance with all Contract Documents.

question_text: What are the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Terms & Conditions?
answer: § 4.3.4 Concealed or Unknown Conditions. If the Contractor encounters conditions at the site that are (1) subsurface or otherwise concealed physical conditions that differ materially from those indicated in the Contract Documents or (2) unknown physical conditions of an unusual nature, that differ materially from those ordinarily found to exist and generally recognized as inherent in construction activities of the character provided for in the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall promptly provide notice to the Owner and the Architect before conditions are disturbed and in no event later than twenty-one (21) days after first observance of the conditions. The Architect will promptly investigate such conditions and, if the Architect determines that they differ materially and cause an increase or decrease in the Contractor’s cost of, or time required for, performance of any part of the Work, will recommend an equitable adjustment in the Contract Sum or Contract Time, or both. question_text: List the Owner / Project Required Reports
answer: CLAUSE L3 MONTHLY STATUS REPORTS (a) The Contractor shall prepare a monthly status report based on a template format provided by Princeton University that contains, without limitation to other requirements for same as may be provided elsewhere in the Contract, the following items: (1) An executive summary consisting of a clear and concise statement of the Contractor’s opinion of the state of the Project, a narrative of the project cost and schedule status, an outline of remedial action plans required by all team members to meet the project cost and schedule requirements and a narrative of the outstanding items of information or decisions that must be made to progress the Work. (2) For GMP Type Contracts: A construction cost report that shows the approved budget, costs committed and paid do date, the anticipated cost for each budget line item and the variance of each budgeted line item and total to the anticipated cost. (3) For CPFF Type Contracts: A construction cost report that shows the then-approved Target Price breakdown, costs committed and paid do date, the anticipated cost for each Target-Price line item and the variance of each budgeted line item and total to the anticipated cost. (4) A significant risks/major issues list with assigned responsibility and planned date for required resolution. (5) A summary of the commitments, anticipated items, and transfers against the Contractor’s Construction Contingency within the GMP budget. (6) A schedule report that compares the baseline schedule to the current version and an explanation of the variance between the schedules. Also include an action plan to recover any negative variance from the approved baseline, an explanation of how past productivity has influenced the updated logic and durations, and explanation(s) for the cause of any delays and the party responsible for the cost of the related recovery efforts. (7) Monthly cash flow and manpower reports that compare an approved baseline to actual and projected cash flows and manpower forecasts. Also, include an explanation of the cause for any variance between the actual/current projections and the baseline. (8) Progress photographs of the Project. (9) Project metrics as deemed important by the entire team such as the number of outstanding shop drawings and RFI’s, Architect/Engineer and Princeton University response time. Also, include a narrative of the metric data and its impact on the Project. Do not include the logs used to track this information. (10) A summary of major outstanding quality issues including schedules and remedies to correct the conditions. (11) A summary of the follow-up actions taken from the previous month’s reports and any outstanding issues that still require action and by which team members. (12) A summary of the posting of digital archiving documents to the Capital Project Management System including as-built drawings, record submittals, maintenance manuals, commissioning reports and warranty letters.
